planting.note: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is federally threatened in the wild but adapts well to cultivation in zones 5–8.

directions: Sow direct in fall for cold stratification and spring germination in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or gritty soil in full sun; Tennessee coneflower is adapted to thin, calcareous, rocky cedar glades in Middle Tennessee and requires excellent drainage and lean soil — avoid rich, heavy soils. Echinacea tennessensis is a federally threatened coneflower endemic to a tiny area of cedar glades in Middle Tennessee; it is distinguished from other echinaceas by its reflexed ray petals that curve strongly downward along the central cone and its preference for alkaline, rocky soils. Despite its limited natural range, it is adaptable in cultivation throughout zones 5–8 in well-drained, lean soils; it is a beautiful, narrow-endemic wildflower of conservation significance and an important ornamental for butterfly gardens. Purchase only nursery-propagated plants.

planting.note: Sow direct in spring after scarifying seeds (nick or sand the hard seed coat) in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; American senna is an impressive native perennial with bold foliage and prolific yellow flowers.

directions: Scarify seeds by nicking the hard seed coat with a file or soaking overnight in hot water, then direct sow in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; American senna is a vigorous grower once established. Senna hebecarpa is a large, bold native perennial of eastern North American moist woodlands and thickets growing to 4–6 feet tall, with large, pinnately compound leaves and clusters of bright yellow, 5-petaled flowers in late summer followed by flat, oblong seed pods eaten by birds; it is the larval food plant of the cloudless sulphur butterfly (Phoebis sennae), the sleepy orange butterfly, and several other sulphur species. The dramatic tropical foliage and prolific golden flowers make it a standout at the back of native plant borders and in naturalized wildlife gardens; it spreads slowly by rhizomes to form clumps.

planting.note: Plant tuber divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone is a delicate spring ephemeral that goes dormant by early summer — plant with ferns to fill the gap.

directions: Plant small tuber clusters in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; rue anemone goes dormant by early summer, so plant near later-emerging hostas or ferns to cover the gap. Anemonella thalictroides (syn. Thalictrum thalictroides) is a diminutive, ethereal native spring ephemeral of eastern North American woodlands; it emerges in early spring with finely divided, three-lobed, glaucous-blue-green leaves resembling miniature meadow rue and bears small (3/4 inch), white to pale pink, 5–10-petaled flowers on delicate, hair-fine stems from March through May, creating a cloud of floating blossoms just inches above the ground. Despite its delicate appearance, it is surprisingly vigorous and long-lived; it spreads slowly by tuberous roots and forms charming colonies under deciduous trees. A very special native wildflower for collectors and connoisseurs of spring woodland plants.

planting.note: Sow direct in fall near the roots of grass species (little bluestem, sideoats grama) for cold stratification; Indian paintbrush is hemiparasitic on grass roots and must be sown in the presence of suitable host grass roots to thrive.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall on the soil surface near the roots of established native grasses such as little bluestem (Schizachyrium scoparium) or sideoats grama (Bouteloua curtipendula); Indian paintbrush is hemiparasitic on the roots of neighboring plants, especially grasses, and MUST have suitable host plant roots to survive — it will not thrive when grown alone. Castilleja coccinea is the eastern native paintbrush, a biennial or short-lived perennial whose brilliant scarlet display comes not from its true flowers but from the brightly colored bracts (modified leaves) surrounding the inconspicuous true flowers; it is one of the most spectacular native wildflowers of prairies and meadows, intensely attractive to ruby-throated hummingbirds. Because of its parasitic requirement, it is challenging to cultivate but rewards the patient gardener who establishes it correctly in a native grass meadow planting. Mix seed with native grass seed and sow together.

planting.note: Plant divisions near suitable host plant roots in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; wood betony is hemiparasitic primarily on grasses and some forbs — it performs best in native plant communities with a diversity of host species.

directions: Plant divisions or carefully transplant container-grown plants in spring near the roots of grasses and native forbs in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade or light dappled sun; wood betony is hemiparasitic and performs best embedded in a diverse native plant community with ample grass and forb roots to tap. Pedicularis canadensis is a native eastern North American hemiparasitic perennial of woodland edges, prairies, and open rocky slopes; it produces attractive, deeply pinnately lobed, fernlike leaves in a basal rosette and stout spikes of clustered, tubular flowers with distinctive, hooded upper lips in colors ranging from yellow through red in mid-spring, one of the most beautiful and architecturally interesting of the spring wildflowers. It is among the most important early-season nectar plants for queen bumblebees emerging from hibernation. Like paintbrush, it benefits from diverse host-plant associations and is most successful in native meadow and woodland garden settings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, peaty, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; goldthread requires cool, moist, acidic conditions and is best in boreal or subalpine woodland gardens with sphagnum moss and native acid-tolerant companions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, peaty, strongly acidic (pH 4.0–5.5), humus-rich or mossy soil in partial to full shade; goldthread requires constantly moist, cool, acidic conditions and grows naturally in sphagnum bogs, mossy boreal forests, and shaded stream margins. Coptis groenlandica (syn. C. trifolia subsp. groenlandica) is a diminutive, evergreen native perennial of boreal North America producing small, glossy, trifoliate leaves resembling miniature strawberry leaves and tiny, white, 5-petaled flowers with prominent, club-shaped golden staminodes in early spring; the bright-yellow thread-like rhizomes (giving rise to the common name) contain berberine and were used medicinally by Native Americans for mouth sores (canker root). It forms a charming, low mat in cool, acidic, bog and woodland gardens alongside pitcher plants, sundews, and native orchids. Best suited to zones 2–6 where cool, consistently moist conditions can be maintained.
